disease.name: Breast Milk Jaundice
disease.note: Late-onset unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in healthy breastfed infants. Onset day 5-14 of life, peaking around day 10-21 and persisting up to 12 weeks. Distinct from breastfeeding jaundice (early onset, day 2-5). Cause is thought to be: epidermal growth factor, beta-glucuronidase, and certain fatty acids in breast milk that inhibit hepatic UGT1A1 activity and increase intestinal bilirubin absorption. BENIGN condition. Management: NO need to stop breastfeeding; phototherapy only if levels exceed Bhutani nomogram threshold for gestational age. Reassurance is key.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Most cases require reassurance only; phototherapy if levels cross threshold

subtypes.name: Typical Breast Milk Jaundice
subtypes.notes: Mild unconjugated jaundice peaking day 10-21, self-resolving by 12 weeks

subtypes.name: Severe Breast Milk Jaundice Requiring Phototherapy
subtypes.notes: Bilirubin >15-20 mg/dL requiring phototherapy despite adequate feeding

subtypes.name: Prolonged Breast Milk Jaundice
subtypes.notes: Jaundice persisting beyond 4-6 weeks, needs workup to exclude biliary atresia and other pathology

firstLine.test: Total and fractionated bilirubin (conjugated vs unconjugated)
Blood type and Rh, direct Coombs

secondLine.test: Direct bilirubin fractionation if prolonged >3 weeks
TSH/fT4 (if prolonged and concern for hypothyroidism)

investigations.goldStandard: Fractionated bilirubin with normal direct fraction, in healthy exclusively breastfed infant with negative hemolysis workup

emergency: Phototherapy if bilirubin above AAP thresholds for gestational age post-natal day
Exchange transfusion if extreme levels or signs of encephalopathy
inpatient: Phototherapy for moderate-severe levels per Bhutani nomogram
Monitor bilirubin q12-24h until downward trend
Ensure adequate hydration and calorie intake
outpatient: Reassurance - this is a benign self-limited condition
Continue exclusive breastfeeding - DO NOT DISCONTINUE
Follow-up bilirubin check weekly until trending down
Stool color monitoring by parents (ensure pigmented)
If jaundice persists >3-4 weeks, fractionated bilirubin and direct fraction to exclude cholestasis
refractory: Consider 48-hour formula break only if bilirubin extremely high and rising despite phototherapy
Home phototherapy for select cases with careful monitoring
preventive: Antenatal education that this is a normal benign condition
Early recognition and monitoring for escalation

disease.pathophysiology: Certain compounds in breast milk have been implicated: epidermal growth factor (EGF) suppresses UGT1A1 activity; beta-glucuronidase in breast milk hydrolyzes bilirubin glucuronides in the infant gut, freeing unconjugated bilirubin for reabsorption via enterohepatic circulation; pregnanediol and free fatty acids (particularly unsaturated) inhibit hepatic bilirubin conjugation. The result is prolonged unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in otherwise healthy breastfed infants due to both decreased hepatic clearance and increased intestinal reabsorption of bilirubin. Unlike breastfeeding jaundice, there is NO inadequate intake or dehydration. Condition is benign and resolves spontaneously as the infant's liver matures and enteral intake includes complementary foods.

complications.complication: Kernicterus (rare - only if severe with phototherapy failure)
Maternal anxiety and unnecessary breastfeeding cessation
Unnecessary invasive workup for prolonged jaundice

mustNotMiss: Direct/conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (>2 mg/dL or >20% of total) - suggests cholestasis, NOT breast milk jaundice
Acholic/pale stools - biliary atresia until proven otherwise
Signs of hypothyroidism (prolonged jaundice + feeding difficulty + constipation + large fontanelles)
Hepatosplenomegaly suggests hemolysis, infection, or metabolic disease
Failure to thrive accompanying jaundice suggests organic etiology beyond breast milk jaundice
Urinary tract infection can present with prolonged jaundice in neonates

diagnosticClues.clue: Healthy-appearing exclusively breastfed infant with jaundice onset day 5-14, peaking day 10-21
Unconjugated (indirect) hyperbilirubinemia with normal direct fraction
Excellent weight gain, adequate wet diapers, pigmented stools
No signs of hemolysis: normal Hb, normal reticulocytes, negative Coombs
Prior sibling with similar prolonged jaundice
Jaundice gradually resolves over 4-12 weeks without specific intervention

exclusionClues.clue: Direct/conjugated bilirubin >2 mg/dL or >20% of total
Weight loss >7% or failure to thrive (suggests breastfeeding jaundice or metabolic disease)
Jaundice appearing before day 3 of life (suggests hemolytic disease or physiological)
Jaundice persisting beyond 12 weeks (needs further evaluation)
